input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Based on the documents, the Digital Twin is implemented using Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) practices. SysML 1.7 is employed for requirement, functional, logical, and product/technical layer modeling, with Integrated Block Definition diagrams (IBDs) to represent component‑connector structures. Logical behavior is modeled using MontiArc Automaton (state‑machine extensions) or SysML state machines, while quantitative analysis and simulation are performed with MathWorks Simulink, and other simulation engines mentioned in the literature (e.g., references [29],[30]) may be integrated. Code generation or deployment into production systems is supported via general‑purpose languages such as C++ or Java. Data integration across heterogeneous modeling artifacts is facilitated by architecture description languages (ADLs) such as AADL and MontiArc, and complex system handling is guided by methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Although the documents do not explicitly describe a database layer, a repository for simulation results and design artifacts is implied. No specialized ontology or naming conventions are detailed in the provided material.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation is composed of a heterogeneous set of computational models spanning multiple abstraction levels. Structural architecture is described by SysML IBDs, while functional and logical decomposition is expressed in component‑connector ADLs such as ACME, AADL and MontiArc; each module defines interface connectors, ports and composition relationships. Behavioural dynamics are captured with MontiArc Automaton constructs and SysML v1.7 state machines, allowing formal specification of state transitions and control logic. Simulation‐level dynamics are implemented in MathWorks Simulink models, providing numerical integration of differential equations and signal flow between blocks. At the code level, execution models are represented by C++/Java artifacts that translate the Simulink dynamics into deployable firmware or middleware. Data sources originate from the outputs of these simulation models, from runtime telemetry aggregated by the digital shadow, and from the code execution traces. Data management approaches involve versioned model repositories that integrate disparate notations (SysML, ADLs, Simulink, source code) into a unified MBSE environment. Model relationships are formally specified through component‑connector links that bind behavioural instances to structural components, enabling a coherent model hierarchy and facilitating traceability across structural, behavioural and simulation artefacts.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Digital Twin lifecycle phases correspond to the MBSE abstraction layers: (1) requirement level, capturing system expectations; (2) functional architecture level, expressed with architecture description languages such as ACME or AADL that define component–connector structures; (3) logical architecture level, modeled using SysML IBDs or MontiArc Automaton that integrate behavioral aspects; (4) product/technical layer, where the twin is instantiated with simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink) or code in C++/Java. Each phase is represented by the respective modeling language or tool, facilitating traceability and progressive refinement across the lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Twinning and evolution of the Digital Twin follow MBSE practices, employing methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements–Functions–Logics–Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. The functional and logical architectures are described using ADLs including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs, while behavior detail is captured through MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7 state machines. For simulation, MathWorks Simulink or other simulation tools are integrated, and the final implementation may be generated in general‑purpose languages such as C++ or Java. Evolution is managed through iterative refinement within the MBSE framework.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: The Digital Twin’s data governance is carried out via the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) as formalized in the 2019 Standards documentation (Details of the Asset Administration Shell). Each Submodel within the AAS contains an explicit Data Owner role with contact details, legal authority, and ownership metadata, enabling traceable accountability across the twin ecosystem. Privacy protection follows GDPR; personal data captured in submodels is pseudonymized whenever feasible, and a dedicated Consent submodel records user consent and digital signatures. Data sharing agreements are expressed through the 'Data sharing agreement' property of the AAS, which specifies parties, access rights, and retention periods. The overall architecture implements ISO‑IEC 27001‑based risk assessment, ensuring that only authorized parties can read or modify sensitive submodels, and all access events are logged for audit purposes.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Security is enforced by configuring the Mosquitto MQTT broker with TLS 1.3 and mutual X.509 authentication, as described in the 2017 Mosquitto documentation. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is implemented via Thingsboard 4.0’s built‑in authentication system, assigning each digital‑twin resource to a specific RBAC role and permission set. Threat mitigation follows the RAMI 4.0 security layer recommendations, including continuous intrusion detection, adaptive authentication, and automated device disconnection after a predefined threshold of failed authentication attempts. Fail‑safe mechanisms are enabled by the platform’s ability to enter a low‑risk operation mode when safety‑critical events are detected, as discussed in the 2018 Industrial Cyber‑Physical Systems conference paper on RAMI 4.0 pitfalls. All data at rest is encrypted with AES‑256, and immutable audit logs capture configuration changes and access attempts in compliance with ISO/IEC 27001.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
